questions.question: It is usually in 4/4 and often uses a 12-bar chord pattern. 8-bar and 16-bar forms are also used. What is this?
questions.hint: Twelve bars, bent notes and call and response.
questions.explanation: It is usually in 4/4 and often uses a 12-bar chord pattern. 8-bar and 16-bar forms are also used.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Blues?

answers.text: It is a staged work combining songs, spoken dialogue, acting and dance.
It is often in 4/4 and often follows a 12-bar chord pattern.
Blue notes, call and response and repeated riffs are common.
Improvisation is an important part of the style.

questions.hint: Spoken dialogue, songs, acting and dancing on stage.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a staged work combining songs, spoken dialogue, acting and dance” describes Musical, not Blues. It is usually in 4/4 and often uses a 12-bar chord pattern. 8-bar and 16-bar forms are also used.

questions.question: Common features are syncopation, improvisation, distinctive harmony and instrumental solos, played by small groups or big bands. What is this?
questions.hint: Syncopation, improvisation and instrumental solos.
questions.explanation: Common features are syncopation, improvisation, distinctive harmony and instrumental solos, played by small groups or big bands.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Jazz?

answers.text: Syncopation and improvisation are common features.
It is a staged work combining songs, spoken dialogue, acting and dance.
Instrumental solos and distinctive harmony are often heard.
Saxophone, trumpet, piano, bass and drum kit are common instruments.

questions.hint: Spoken dialogue, songs, acting and dancing on stage.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a staged work combining songs, spoken dialogue, acting and dance” describes Musical, not Jazz. Common features are syncopation, improvisation, distinctive harmony and instrumental solos, played by small groups or big bands.

questions.question: Dance music from Latin America, often with lively syncopated or off-beat rhythms, a strong pulse and clear percussion. What is this?
questions.hint: Think salsa rhythms, bongos and claves.
questions.explanation: Dance music from Latin America, often with lively syncopated or off-beat rhythms, a strong pulse and clear percussion.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Latin American?

answers.text: It often has lively syncopated or off-beat rhythms.
A strong dance pulse and prominent percussion are common.
Distorted electric-guitar riffs and a heavy amplified sound are central.
Bongos, guiro, castanets or claves may be used.

questions.hint: Amplified guitars, a driving beat and distortion.
questions.explanation: The statement “Distorted electric-guitar riffs and a heavy amplified sound are central” describes Rock, not Latin American. Dance music from Latin America, often with lively syncopated or off-beat rhythms, a strong pulse and clear percussion.

questions.question: A stage show with speaking, songs, acting and usually dancing. What is this?
questions.hint: Spoken dialogue, songs, acting and dancing on stage.
questions.explanation: A stage show with speaking, songs, acting and usually dancing.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Musical?

answers.text: It is a staged dramatic work with songs, acting and usually dance.
Spoken dialogue may be used between songs.
A band or orchestra accompanies songs that develop the story or characters.
Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.

questions.hint: Buzzing lips meet a cup-shaped mouthpiece.
questions.explanation: The statement “Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece” describes Brass, not Musical. A stage show with speaking, songs, acting and usually dancing.

questions.question: Commercial popular music built around memorable songs, clear hooks and repeated sections such as verses and choruses. What is this?
questions.hint: Catchy hooks, verses and a returning chorus.
questions.explanation: Commercial popular music built around memorable songs, clear hooks and repeated sections such as verses and choruses.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pop?

answers.text: It is performed by tuned steel pans associated with Trinidad and Tobago.
Memorable songs and clear hooks are important.
Verses and choruses are often repeated.
Lead vocals are often supported by guitars, keyboards, bass and drum kit.

questions.hint: Tuned Caribbean pans shaped from metal.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is performed by tuned steel pans associated with Trinidad and Tobago” describes Steel band, not Pop. Commercial popular music built around memorable songs, clear hooks and repeated sections such as verses and choruses.

questions.question: A popular style with a strong driving beat and loud amplified sound. What is this?
questions.hint: Amplified guitars, a driving beat and distortion.
questions.explanation: A popular style with a strong driving beat and loud amplified sound.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Rock?

answers.text: It has a strong driving beat and prominent amplified sound.
It is a large ensemble divided into strings, woodwind, brass and percussion.
Electric guitar, bass guitar, drum kit and vocals are common.
Guitar riffs and distortion are often important.

questions.hint: A conductor unites four instrument families in one large ensemble.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a large ensemble divided into strings, woodwind, brass and percussion” describes Orchestra, not Rock. A popular style with a strong driving beat and loud amplified sound.

questions.question: A lively American style combining elements of blues, rhythm and blues, gospel and country. What is this?
questions.hint: Strong backbeat, walking bass and a 12-bar pattern.
questions.explanation: A lively American style combining elements of blues, rhythm and blues, gospel and country.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Rock 'n' roll?

answers.text: It is a lively American style with roots in blues, gospel and country.
A strong backbeat and walking bass are common.
It is a dance in simple triple time with a ONE-two-three accent pattern.
Riffs and a 12-bar chord pattern are often used.

questions.hint: ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a dance in simple triple time with a ONE-two-three accent pattern” describes Waltz, not Rock 'n' roll. A lively American style combining elements of blues, rhythm and blues, gospel and country.

questions.question: Traditional songs, dances and instrumental music from Scotland. What is this?
questions.hint: Fiddles, bagpipes, reels and strathspeys.
questions.explanation: Traditional songs, dances and instrumental music from Scotland.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Scottish?

answers.text: It includes traditional songs, dances and instrumental music from Scotland.
Fiddle, accordion and bagpipes are common instruments.
Marches, reels, jigs, strathspeys and waltzes are common dance types.
Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.

questions.hint: Buzzing lips meet a cup-shaped mouthpiece.
questions.explanation: The statement “Sound is made by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece” describes Brass, not Scottish. Traditional songs, dances and instrumental music from Scotland.

questions.question: Pitch moves upwards, so each later note is higher than the note before it. What is this?
questions.hint: Every new pitch climbs higher.
questions.explanation: Pitch moves upwards, so each later note is higher than the note before it.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Ascending?

answers.text: Each later pitch is lower than the one before it.
Each later pitch is higher than the one before it.
A melody travels from a lower register towards a higher one.
The upward movement may happen by step or by leap.

questions.hint: Every new pitch travels lower.
questions.explanation: The statement “Each later pitch is lower than the one before it” describes Descending, not Ascending. Pitch moves upwards, so each later note is higher than the note before it.

questions.question: Two or more different notes played together. What is this?
questions.hint: A guitarist may strum one to support a tune.
questions.explanation: Two or more different notes played together.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Chord?

answers.text: Two or more different notes sound together.
It is the interval between notes with the same letter name.
It creates harmony and can support or accompany a melody.
A three-note example is called a triad.

questions.hint: C to the next C: eight scale degrees.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the interval between notes with the same letter name” describes Octave, not Chord. Two or more different notes played together.

questions.question: A movement from one chord to another. What is this?
questions.hint: Harmony moves from one group of notes to another.
questions.explanation: A movement from one chord to another.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Chord change?

answers.text: It is a movement from one chord to another.
A series of these movements creates a chord progression.
The tempo decreases so that the beat moves less quickly than before.
Related chords often share notes from the same scale.

questions.hint: The tempo eases to a less hurried pace.
questions.explanation: The statement “The tempo decreases so that the beat moves less quickly than before” describes Slower, not Chord change. A movement from one chord to another.

questions.question: Pitch moves downwards, so each later note is lower than the note before it. What is this?
questions.hint: Every new pitch travels lower.
questions.explanation: Pitch moves downwards, so each later note is lower than the note before it.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Descending?

answers.text: Each later pitch is lower than the one before it.
A melody travels from a higher register towards a lower one.
The downward movement may happen by step or by leap.
Each later pitch is higher than the one before it.

questions.hint: Every new pitch climbs higher.
questions.explanation: The statement “Each later pitch is higher than the one before it” describes Ascending, not Descending. Pitch moves downwards, so each later note is lower than the note before it.

questions.question: A combination of notes that clash and create tension or instability. What is this?
questions.hint: Clashing notes create tension before resolving.
questions.explanation: A combination of notes that clash and create tension or instability.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Discord?

answers.text: All performers sing or play the same melody at the same pitch.
Notes clash when they sound together.
The sound creates tension or instability.
It may resolve to a smoother, more settled chord.

questions.hint: Many performers, one melody, same pitch.
questions.explanation: The statement “All performers sing or play the same melody at the same pitch” describes Unison, not Discord. A combination of notes that clash and create tension or instability.

questions.question: The performer makes up music while playing instead of following a fully written part. What is this?
questions.hint: Made up during the performance.
questions.explanation: The performer makes up music while playing instead of following a fully written part.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Improvisation?

answers.text: Musical material is created during the performance.
A written dot and curved arch tells the performer to hold a note or rest longer.
Chords, scales or an agreed structure may guide the performer.
It is common in jazz and blues.

questions.hint: A curved arch and dot: hold it longer.
questions.explanation: The statement “A written dot and curved arch tells the performer to hold a note or rest longer” describes Pause, not Improvisation. The performer makes up music while playing instead of following a fully written part.

questions.question: A melody moves between notes that are not next to one another. What is this?
questions.hint: A _____ of faith.
questions.explanation: A melody moves between notes that are not next to one another.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Leap / leaping?

answers.text: The melody moves between notes that are not next to each other.
One or more neighbouring scale notes are skipped.
The melody moves only between neighbouring notes of the scale.
It creates a larger pitch movement than a step.

questions.hint: Move only to a neighbouring scale note.
questions.explanation: The statement “The melody moves only between neighbouring notes of the scale” describes Step / stepwise, not Leap / leaping. A melody moves between notes that are not next to one another.

questions.question: An opening musical phrase is followed by a responding phrase. What is this?
questions.hint: One phrase calls; the next replies.
questions.explanation: An opening musical phrase is followed by a responding phrase.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Question and answer?

answers.text: An opening phrase is followed by a responding phrase.
The two phrases are often similar in length or rhythm.
The response may complete, contrast with or copy the opening.
One short pattern repeats continuously beneath the music.

questions.hint: A short pattern loops while the music continues around it.
questions.explanation: The statement “One short pattern repeats continuously beneath the music” describes Ostinato / riff, not Question and answer. An opening musical phrase is followed by a responding phrase.

questions.question: A musical idea returns without changing its pitch pattern or rhythm. What is this?
questions.hint: A musical idea returns exactly, but need not keep looping.
questions.explanation: A musical idea returns without changing its pitch pattern or rhythm.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Repetition?

answers.text: The repeated phrase begins on a new higher or lower pitch.
A musical idea returns without changing its pitch pattern or rhythm.
It may happen in a melody or rhythm.
It may also shape the structure of a piece.

questions.hint: Same melodic shape, new starting pitch.
questions.explanation: The statement “The repeated phrase begins on a new higher or lower pitch” describes Sequence, not Repetition. A musical idea returns without changing its pitch pattern or rhythm.

questions.question: A melodic phrase is immediately repeated starting on a higher or lower pitch. What is this?
questions.hint: Same melodic shape, new starting pitch.
questions.explanation: A melodic phrase is immediately repeated starting on a higher or lower pitch.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Sequence?

answers.text: A melodic phrase is repeated immediately.
The idea repeats without any change to its pitch pattern.
The repeat begins on a higher or lower pitch.
The phrase keeps its shape while its pitch level changes.

questions.hint: A musical idea returns exactly, but need not keep looping.
questions.explanation: The statement “The idea repeats without any change to its pitch pattern” describes Repetition, not Sequence. A melodic phrase is immediately repeated starting on a higher or lower pitch.

questions.question: A melody moves between neighbouring notes of a scale. What is this?
questions.hint: Move only to a neighbouring scale note.
questions.explanation: A melody moves between neighbouring notes of a scale.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Step / stepwise?

answers.text: The melody moves between neighbouring notes of a scale.
It is the smallest normal movement within that scale.
The melody skips one or more neighbouring scale notes.
No scale note is skipped between the two pitches.

questions.hint: A _____ of faith.
questions.explanation: The statement “The melody skips one or more neighbouring scale notes” describes Leap / leaping, not Step / stepwise. A melody moves between neighbouring notes of a scale.

questions.question: Which is most common?
questions.hint: Even number
questions.explanation: 4 beats per bar is the most common. A 4/4 time signature is sometimes called common time.

questions.question: How many beats per bar does a Waltz have?
questions.hint: Oom pah pah
questions.explanation: A waltz has 3 beats per bar.

questions.question: Which time signature has 3 beats per bar?
questions.hint: The first number tells you how many beats per bar there are.
questions.explanation: 3/4 has 3 beats per par.

questions.question: A note or beat receives extra emphasis, so it stands out from those around it. What is this?
questions.hint: A > sign gives one note extra emphasis.
questions.explanation: A note or beat receives extra emphasis, so it stands out from those around it.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of an accent?

answers.text: Tells the performer to hold a note longer
A note or beat receives extra emphasis
The sound may be made stronger or louder than those around it

questions.hint: An accent adds extra emphasis to a note.
questions.explanation: The statement “A dot beneath a curved arch tells the performer to hold a note longer” describes Pause, not Accent / accented. A note or beat receives extra emphasis, so it stands out from those around it.

questions.question: A slow tempo marking. What is this?
questions.hint: Italian for a calm, slow pace.
questions.explanation: A slow tempo marking.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Adagio?

answers.text: It is a slow tempo marking.
It is a fast, lively marking often linked with 120-168 beats per minute.
The music should move at a calm, unhurried speed.
It is often associated with about 66-76 beats per minute.

questions.hint: Italian for fast and lively.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a fast, lively marking often linked with 120-168 beats per minute” describes Allegro, not Adagio. A slow tempo marking.

questions.question: A fast and lively tempo marking. What is this?
questions.hint: Italian for fast and lively.
questions.explanation: A fast and lively tempo marking.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Allegro?

answers.text: It is a fast and lively tempo marking.
The music should move with energy and forward motion.
It is a slow marking linked with a calm, unhurried speed.
It is often associated with about 120-168 beats per minute.

questions.hint: Italian for a calm, slow pace.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a slow marking linked with a calm, unhurried speed” describes Adagio, not Allegro. A fast and lively tempo marking.

questions.question: The steady pattern that you can tap or clap along with. What is this?
questions.hint: The steady tap beneath the music.
questions.explanation: The steady pattern that you can tap or clap along with.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Beat / pulse?

answers.text: It is the regular underlying pulse of the music.
A listener might tap or clap along with it.
Tempo tells us how quickly it moves.
It is a short rhythmic decoration played on a drum kit near a section change.

questions.hint: A brief kit flourish between phrases.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a short rhythmic decoration played on a drum kit near a section change” describes Drum fill, not Beat / pulse. The steady pattern that you can tap or clap along with.

questions.question: A short rhythmic decoration played on the drum kit, often near the end of a phrase or section. What is this?
questions.hint: A brief kit flourish between phrases.
questions.explanation: A short rhythmic decoration played on the drum kit, often near the end of a phrase or section.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Drum fill?

answers.text: It is the regular underlying pulse that a listener can tap along with.
It is a short rhythmic decoration played on a drum kit.
It often appears near the end of a phrase or section.
It may signal a change or add excitement.

questions.hint: The steady tap beneath the music.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the regular underlying pulse that a listener can tap along with” describes Beat / pulse, not Drum fill. A short rhythmic decoration played on the drum kit, often near the end of a phrase or section.

questions.question: The tempo increases, so the beat moves more quickly than before. What is this?
questions.hint: The tempo moves more quickly than before.
questions.explanation: The tempo increases, so the beat moves more quickly than before.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Faster?

answers.text: The beat moves less quickly than before.
The beat moves more quickly than before.
The change may happen suddenly or gradually.

questions.hint: The tempo eases to a less hurried pace.
questions.explanation: The statement “The beat moves less quickly than before” describes Slower, not Faster. The tempo increases, so the beat moves more quickly than before.

questions.question: Music with a strong, regular walking pulse, usually organised in two or four beats per bar. What is this?
questions.hint: LEFT-right, LEFT-right: music for moving together.
questions.explanation: Music with a strong, regular walking pulse, usually organised in two or four beats per bar.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of March?

answers.text: It has a strong, regular walking pulse.
It is usually organised in two or four beats per bar.
It is a simple-triple dance with a ONE-two-three pattern.
Clear accents and percussion often support coordinated movement.

questions.hint: ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a simple-triple dance with a ONE-two-three pattern” describes Waltz, not March. Music with a strong, regular walking pulse, usually organised in two or four beats per bar.

questions.question: Notes or accents fall between the main beats or on their weaker parts. What is this?
questions.hint: Accents land between the main pulses.
questions.explanation: Notes or accents fall between the main beats or on their weaker parts.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Off the beat?

answers.text: Notes or accents fall between or on weaker parts of the main beats.
It can create lift, surprise or syncopated energy.
In 4/4 it may emphasise beats 2 and 4 instead of beats 1 and 3.
Notes and accents line up directly with the main pulse.

questions.hint: The stress lands exactly where you tap your foot.
questions.explanation: The statement “Notes and accents line up directly with the main pulse” describes On the beat, not Off the beat. Notes or accents fall between the main beats or on their weaker parts.

questions.question: Notes or accents fall exactly on the main beat. What is this?
questions.hint: The stress lands exactly where you tap your foot.
questions.explanation: Notes or accents fall exactly on the main beat.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of On the beat?

answers.text: Notes or accents fall between the main beats or on their weaker parts.
Notes or accents line up directly with the main pulse.
It reinforces the metre and often feels rhythmically stable.
In 4/4 it may strengthen beats 1 and 3.

questions.hint: Accents land between the main pulses.
questions.explanation: The statement “Notes or accents fall between the main beats or on their weaker parts” describes Off the beat, not On the beat. Notes or accents fall exactly on the main beat.

questions.question: A sign placed over a note or rest instructing the performer to hold it longer than its written value. What is this?
questions.hint: A curved arch and dot: hold it longer.
questions.explanation: A sign placed over a note or rest instructing the performer to hold it longer than its written value.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pause?

answers.text: A note or rest is held longer than its written value.
A > sign tells the performer to give a note extra emphasis.
The performer or conductor decides the extra length.
Its symbol is a dot beneath a curved arch.

questions.hint: A > sign gives one note extra emphasis.
questions.explanation: The statement “A > sign tells the performer to give a note extra emphasis” describes Accent / accented, not Pause. A sign placed over a note or rest instructing the performer to hold it longer than its written value.

questions.question: A lively Scottish dance in simple time, usually with two or four beats per bar. What is this?
questions.hint: A quick Scottish dance in two or four.
questions.explanation: A lively Scottish dance in simple time, usually with two or four beats per bar.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Reel?

answers.text: It is a lively Scottish dance in simple time.
It usually has two or four beats per bar and a quick, flowing pulse.
It is a triple-time dance with a strong ONE-two-three pattern.
Fiddle, accordion or a Scottish dance band often performs it.

questions.hint: ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a triple-time dance with a strong ONE-two-three pattern” describes Waltz, not Reel. A lively Scottish dance in simple time, usually with two or four beats per bar.

questions.question: The tempo decreases, so the beat moves less quickly than before. What is this?
questions.hint: The tempo eases to a less hurried pace.
questions.explanation: The tempo decreases, so the beat moves less quickly than before.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Slower?

answers.text: The beat moves less quickly than before.
The change may happen suddenly or gradually.
The beat moves more quickly than before.

questions.hint: The tempo moves more quickly than before.
questions.explanation: The statement “The beat moves more quickly than before” describes Faster, not Slower. The tempo decreases, so the beat moves less quickly than before.

questions.question: A dance in simple triple time with three beats in each bar. What is this?
questions.hint: ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three.
questions.explanation: A dance in simple triple time with three beats in each bar.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Waltz?

answers.text: It has a strong walking pulse with two or four beats per bar.
It is a dance in simple triple time.
There are three beats in each bar.
The first beat is strongest, creating a ONE-two-three pattern.

questions.hint: LEFT-right, LEFT-right: music for moving together.
questions.explanation: The statement “It has a strong walking pulse with two or four beats per bar” describes March, not Waltz. A dance in simple triple time with three beats in each bar.

questions.question: A main voice or instrument is supported by additional voices or instruments. What is this?
questions.hint: The main performer has musical support.
questions.explanation: A main voice or instrument is supported by additional voices or instruments.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Accompanied?

answers.text: A main voice or instrument receives musical support.
The performer has no supporting instruments or harmony parts.
Other voices or instruments provide the support.
The supporting part may add harmony, rhythm or texture.

questions.hint: No instruments or supporting parts join in.
questions.explanation: The statement “The performer has no supporting instruments or harmony parts” describes Unaccompanied, not Accompanied. A main voice or instrument is supported by additional voices or instruments.

questions.question: In chordal or multi-part music, two or more different notes sound at the same time. What is this?
questions.hint: Backing singers add different notes above or below the main tune.
questions.explanation: In chordal or multi-part music, two or more different notes sound at the same time.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Harmony?

answers.text: Two or more different notes sound at the same time.
Every performer plays the same melody at the same pitch and time.
Independent musical parts can combine to create it.

questions.hint: Many performers, one melody, same pitch.
questions.explanation: The statement “Every performer plays the same melody at the same pitch and time” describes Unison, not Harmony. In chordal or multi-part music, two or more different notes sound at the same time.

questions.question: The interval from a note to the next higher or lower note with the same letter name, such as C to C. What is this?
questions.hint: C to the next C: eight scale degrees.
questions.explanation: The interval from a note to the next higher or lower note with the same letter name, such as C to C.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Octave?

answers.text: The two notes have the same letter name.
One note is the next higher or lower version of the other.
The notes sound closely related at different pitch levels.
A three-note chord is called a triad.

questions.hint: A guitarist may strum one to support a tune.
questions.explanation: The statement “A three-note chord is called a triad” describes Chord, not Octave. The interval from a note to the next higher or lower note with the same letter name, such as C to C.

questions.question: A short musical pattern or phrase repeated many times. What is this?
questions.hint: A short pattern loops while the music continues around it.
questions.explanation: A short musical pattern or phrase repeated many times.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Ostinato / riff?

answers.text: A phrase repeats immediately at a different pitch level.
A short musical pattern or phrase repeats many times.
The repeated idea may contain rhythm, pitches or both.
A memorable repeated instrumental phrase is often called a riff.

questions.hint: Same melodic shape, new starting pitch.
questions.explanation: The statement “A phrase repeats immediately at a different pitch level” describes Sequence, not Ostinato / riff. A short musical pattern or phrase repeated many times.

questions.question: All parts perform the same melody but begin at different times. What is this?
questions.hint: The same tune begins at different times.
questions.explanation: All parts perform the same melody but begin at different times.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Round?

answers.text: All parts perform the same melody.
All parts perform the same melody at the same pitch and at the same time.
The parts begin that melody at different times.
The melody overlaps as each part continues and starts again.

questions.hint: Many performers, one melody, same pitch.
questions.explanation: The statement “All parts perform the same melody at the same pitch and at the same time” describes Unison, not Round. All parts perform the same melody but begin at different times.

questions.question: One voice or instrument performs alone or has the main part. What is this?
questions.hint: One performer takes the spotlight.
questions.explanation: One voice or instrument performs alone or has the main part.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Solo?

answers.text: One voice or instrument takes the most prominent part.
It may be performed completely alone.
A whole organised group of singers performs together.
It may have accompaniment while remaining the featured line.

questions.hint: Soprano, alto, tenor and bass sing together.
questions.explanation: The statement “A whole organised group of singers performs together” describes Choir, not Solo. One voice or instrument performs alone or has the main part.

questions.question: A voice or instrument performs without supporting instruments or harmony parts. What is this?
questions.hint: No instruments or supporting parts join in.
questions.explanation: A voice or instrument performs without supporting instruments or harmony parts.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Unaccompanied?

answers.text: A voice or instrument performs without supporting instruments.
No supporting harmony part is present.
For voices, the more advanced term is a cappella.
Other voices or instruments provide harmony, rhythm or texture underneath.

questions.hint: The main performer has musical support.
questions.explanation: The statement “Other voices or instruments provide harmony, rhythm or texture underneath” describes Accompanied, not Unaccompanied. A voice or instrument performs without supporting instruments or harmony parts.

questions.question: Two or more voices or instruments perform the same melody at the same pitch at the same time. What is this?
questions.hint: Many performers, one melody, same pitch.
questions.explanation: Two or more voices or instruments perform the same melody at the same pitch at the same time.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Unison?

answers.text: Parts begin the same melody at different times so that it overlaps.
Two or more performers play or sing the same melody.
They use the same pitch at the same time.
The result is one melodic line rather than harmony.

questions.hint: The same tune begins at different times.
questions.explanation: The statement “Parts begin the same melody at different times so that it overlaps” describes Round, not Unison. Two or more voices or instruments perform the same melody at the same pitch at the same time.

questions.question: A free-reed instrument sounded by opening and closing bellows. What is this?
questions.hint: Opening and closing bellows push air through reeds.
questions.explanation: A free-reed instrument sounded by opening and closing bellows.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Accordion?

answers.text: Opening and closing bellows push air through free reeds.
Felt-covered hammers strike strings when its keyboard keys are pressed.
The right hand often plays keys or buttons.
The left hand often operates bass and chord buttons.

questions.hint: Felt hammers strike strings when keys are pressed.
questions.explanation: The statement “Felt-covered hammers strike strings when its keyboard keys are pressed” describes Piano, not Accordion. A free-reed instrument sounded by opening and closing bellows.

questions.question: A six-string guitar with a hollow body that resonates and amplifies the vibrating strings without electricity. What is this?
questions.hint: Six strings and a hollow wooden body—no electricity.
questions.explanation: A six-string guitar with a hollow body that resonates and amplifies the vibrating strings without electricity.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Acoustic guitar?

answers.text: Its hollow body amplifies vibrating strings without electricity.
It has a solid or semi-hollow body and needs an amplifier.
It can be plucked or strummed with fingers or a plectrum.

questions.hint: Pickups send vibrating strings to an amplifier.
questions.explanation: The statement “It has a solid or semi-hollow body and needs an amplifier” describes Electric guitar, not Acoustic guitar. A six-string guitar with a hollow body that resonates and amplifies the vibrating strings without electricity.

questions.question: A wind instrument with an air-filled bag, a chanter for the melody and one or more drone pipes that sustain continuous notes. What is this?
questions.hint: A chanter plays the tune above long, steady background notes.
questions.explanation: A wind instrument with an air-filled bag, a chanter for the melody and one or more drone pipes that sustain continuous notes.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Bagpipes?

answers.text: An air-filled bag supplies the sound.
A chanter plays the melody while drone pipes sustain notes.
The chanter uses a double reed.
Its tuned metal playing areas are struck to produce melody, harmony and bass parts.

questions.hint: Tuned Caribbean pans shaped from metal.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its tuned metal playing areas are struck to produce melody, harmony and bass parts” describes Steel band, not Bagpipes. A wind instrument with an air-filled bag, a chanter for the melody and one or more drone pipes that sustain continuous notes.

questions.question: A performer directs air into or across an instrument to make it sound. What is this?
questions.hint: Air travels into or across the instrument.
questions.explanation: A performer directs air into or across an instrument to make it sound.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Blowing?

answers.text: A hand, stick, mallet or beater hits the instrument to make it sound.
The performer directs air into or across an instrument.
Vibrating lips can begin the sound on brass instruments.
An air edge or reed can begin the sound on woodwind instruments.

questions.hint: A hand, stick or mallet makes sudden contact.
questions.explanation: The statement “A hand, stick, mallet or beater hits the instrument to make it sound” describes Striking / hitting, not Blowing. A performer directs air into or across an instrument to make it sound.

questions.question: A performer moves stretched horsehair across the strings of a violin, viola, cello or double bass. What is this?
questions.hint: Horsehair moves across strings for a sustained sound.
questions.explanation: A performer moves stretched horsehair across the strings of a violin, viola, cello or double bass.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Bowing?

answers.text: A bow is drawn across an instrument's strings.
A finger or plectrum pulls and releases the string.
Friction between bow hair and string creates a sustained tone.
The Italian instruction arco tells a string player to use it.

questions.hint: Pull and release one string with a finger or plectrum.
questions.explanation: The statement “A finger or plectrum pulls and releases the string” describes Plucking, not Bowing. A performer moves stretched horsehair across the strings of a violin, viola, cello or double bass.

questions.question: An orchestral family sounded by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece. What is this?
questions.hint: Buzzing lips meet a cup-shaped mouthpiece.
questions.explanation: An orchestral family sounded by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Brass?

answers.text: Buzzing lips make the sound through a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.
Trumpet, French horn, trombone and tuba are members.
Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a single or double reed.
Lip tension, valves or a slide help to change pitch.

questions.hint: Flute air edges, clarinet reeds and oboe double reeds.
questions.explanation: The statement “Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a single or double reed” describes Woodwind, not Brass. An orchestral family sounded by buzzing the lips into a cup-shaped or funnel-shaped mouthpiece.

questions.question: An organised group of singers performing together. What is this?
questions.hint: Soprano, alto, tenor and bass sing together.
questions.explanation: An organised group of singers performing together.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Choir?

answers.text: It is an organised group of singers.
The singers may perform in unison or in several voice parts.
It may sing with or without instrumental accompaniment.
Its members include trumpet, French horn, trombone and tuba.

questions.hint: Buzzing lips meet a cup-shaped mouthpiece.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its members include trumpet, French horn, trombone and tuba” describes Brass, not Choir. An organised group of singers performing together.

questions.question: A collection of drums and cymbals played by one performer using sticks, brushes and foot pedals. What is this?
questions.hint: One player controls cymbals, toms and foot pedals.
questions.explanation: A collection of drums and cymbals played by one performer using sticks, brushes and foot pedals.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Drum kit?

answers.text: A keyboard makes felt-covered hammers strike tuned strings.
One performer plays a collection of drums and cymbals.
Sticks, brushes and foot pedals are used.
Bass drum, snare drum, tom-toms and hi-hat are common parts.

questions.hint: Felt hammers strike strings when keys are pressed.
questions.explanation: The statement “A keyboard makes felt-covered hammers strike tuned strings” describes Piano, not Drum kit. A collection of drums and cymbals played by one performer using sticks, brushes and foot pedals.

questions.question: A string instrument that uses pickups to send its sound to an amplifier. What is this?
questions.hint: Pickups send vibrating strings to an amplifier.
questions.explanation: A string instrument that uses pickups to send its sound to an amplifier.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Electric guitar?

answers.text: Pickups turn string vibrations into an electrical signal.
It is designed to make its full sound without pickups or electrical amplification.
Effects such as distortion may change its sound.

questions.hint: Six strings and a hollow wooden body—no electricity.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is designed to make its full sound without pickups or electrical amplification” describes Acoustic guitar, not Electric guitar. A string instrument that uses pickups to send its sound to an amplifier.

questions.question: A violin used in traditional or folk music, especially Scottish music. What is this?
questions.hint: Held under the chin, bowed, and at home in folk music.
questions.explanation: A violin used in traditional or folk music, especially Scottish music.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Fiddle?

answers.text: It is another name for a violin used in traditional or folk music.
It is especially associated with Scottish music.
It has an air-filled bag, a melody chanter and continuous drone pipes.
It may be bowed or plucked.

questions.hint: A chanter plays the tune above long, steady background notes.
questions.explanation: The statement “It has an air-filled bag, a melody chanter and continuous drone pipes” describes Bagpipes, not Fiddle. A violin used in traditional or folk music, especially Scottish music.

questions.question: A small ensemble that performs traditional or folk music, often using acoustic instruments and voice. What is this?
questions.hint: Traditional voices with fiddle, whistle and acoustic instruments.
questions.explanation: A small ensemble that performs traditional or folk music, often using acoustic instruments and voice.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Folk group?

answers.text: It is a small ensemble performing traditional or folk music.
Acoustic instruments and voices are often used.
Fiddle, whistle, guitar, accordion or bagpipes may be included.
It is divided into strings, woodwind, brass and percussion sections.

questions.hint: A conductor unites four instrument families in one large ensemble.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is divided into strings, woodwind, brass and percussion sections” describes Orchestra, not Folk group. A small ensemble that performs traditional or folk music, often using acoustic instruments and voice.

questions.question: Notes are played or sung smoothly and connected, with little or no audible gap between them. What is this?
questions.hint: Smoothly joined, with no gaps between notes.
questions.explanation: Notes are played or sung smoothly and connected, with little or no audible gap between them.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Legato?

answers.text: Notes are short and detached with clear gaps between them.
Notes are played or sung smoothly.
Each note connects to the next.
There is little or no audible gap between notes.

questions.hint: Short, detached notes marked with dots.
questions.explanation: The statement “Notes are short and detached with clear gaps between them” describes Staccato, not Legato. Notes are played or sung smoothly and connected, with little or no audible gap between them.

questions.question: A large ensemble divided into four main families: strings, woodwind, brass and percussion. What is this?
questions.hint: A conductor unites four instrument families in one large ensemble.
questions.explanation: A large ensemble divided into four main families: strings, woodwind, brass and percussion.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Orchestra?

answers.text: It is a large instrumental ensemble.
It is an organised group of singers performing together.
Its four main families are strings, woodwind, brass and percussion.
Its balance and number of instruments can change with the music.

questions.hint: Soprano, alto, tenor and bass sing together.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is an organised group of singers performing together” describes Choir, not Orchestra. A large ensemble divided into four main families: strings, woodwind, brass and percussion.

questions.question: A keyboard instrument that makes long sounds using pipes or electronic sound generators. What is this?
questions.hint: Church keyboards, pipes, manuals and a pedalboard.
questions.explanation: A keyboard instrument that makes long sounds using pipes or electronic sound generators.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Organ?

answers.text: Its keyboard can produce long sustained tones.
Pipe versions are strongly associated with churches.
Felt-covered hammers strike strings whenever its keys are pressed.
Large examples may have several manuals and a pedalboard.

questions.hint: Felt hammers strike strings when keys are pressed.
questions.explanation: The statement “Felt-covered hammers strike strings whenever its keys are pressed” describes Piano, not Organ. A keyboard instrument that makes long sounds using pipes or electronic sound generators.

questions.question: A family of instruments played mainly by striking, shaking or scraping. What is this?
questions.hint: Shake it, scrape it or strike it.
questions.explanation: A family of instruments played mainly by striking, shaking or scraping.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Percussion?

answers.text: Instruments are sounded mainly by striking, shaking or scraping.
Some members produce definite, identifiable pitches.
Other members produce sounds with no fixed pitch.
Members make sound by buzzing the lips into a mouthpiece.

questions.hint: Buzzing lips meet a cup-shaped mouthpiece.
questions.explanation: The statement “Members make sound by buzzing the lips into a mouthpiece” describes Brass, not Percussion. A family of instruments played mainly by striking, shaking or scraping.

questions.question: As a keyboard instrument, pressing a key causes a felt-covered hammer to strike a string. What is this?
questions.hint: Felt hammers strike strings when keys are pressed.
questions.explanation: As a keyboard instrument, pressing a key causes a felt-covered hammer to strike a string.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Piano?

answers.text: Opening and closing bellows push air through free reeds.
Pressing a key makes a felt-covered hammer strike a string.
It can play melody and harmony together.
Changes in touch create a wide dynamic range.

questions.hint: Opening and closing bellows push air through reeds.
questions.explanation: The statement “Opening and closing bellows push air through free reeds” describes Accordion, not Piano. As a keyboard instrument, pressing a key causes a felt-covered hammer to strike a string.

questions.question: The technique gives each note a clear start and is used on guitar, harp and bowed strings. What is this?
questions.hint: Pull and release one string with a finger or plectrum.
questions.explanation: The technique gives each note a clear start and is used on guitar, harp and bowed strings.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Plucking?

answers.text: A string is pulled and released.
Bow hair rubs against a string to make a sustained tone.
A finger, fingernail or plectrum may be used.
It creates a clear attack on guitar, harp or bowed strings.

questions.hint: Horsehair moves across strings for a sustained sound.
questions.explanation: The statement “Bow hair rubs against a string to make a sustained tone” describes Bowing, not Plucking. The technique gives each note a clear start and is used on guitar, harp and bowed strings.

questions.question: A common group includes accordion, fiddle, piano, bass and drum kit, though the exact instrumentation can vary. What is this?
questions.hint: Accordion and fiddle lead music for ceilidh dancing.
questions.explanation: A common group includes accordion, fiddle, piano, bass and drum kit, though the exact instrumentation can vary.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Scottish dance band?

answers.text: It plays Scottish dance music for social dancing.
Accordion and fiddle are common members.
It uses tuned steel pans to provide melody, harmony and bass parts.
Piano, bass and drum kit may also be included.

questions.hint: Tuned Caribbean pans shaped from metal.
questions.explanation: The statement “It uses tuned steel pans to provide melody, harmony and bass parts” describes Steel band, not Scottish dance band. A common group includes accordion, fiddle, piano, bass and drum kit, though the exact instrumentation can vary.

questions.question: Notes are performed short and detached, with a clear gap or separation between them. What is this?
questions.hint: Short, detached notes marked with dots.
questions.explanation: Notes are performed short and detached, with a clear gap or separation between them.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Staccato?

answers.text: Notes are performed short and detached.
A clear gap separates one note from the next.
A dot above or below the note is common notation for it.
Notes connect smoothly with little or no audible gap.

questions.hint: Smoothly joined, with no gaps between notes.
questions.explanation: The statement “Notes connect smoothly with little or no audible gap” describes Legato, not Staccato. Notes are performed short and detached, with a clear gap or separation between them.

questions.question: Different sizes of pan provide melody, harmony and bass parts. What is this?
questions.hint: Tuned Caribbean pans shaped from metal.
questions.explanation: Different sizes of pan provide melody, harmony and bass parts.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Steel band?

answers.text: Its players use accordions and fiddles for Scottish social dancing.
It is an ensemble of tuned steel pans.
It is associated with Trinidad and Tobago and the Caribbean.
Different pan sizes provide melody, harmony and bass parts.

questions.hint: Accordion and fiddle lead music for ceilidh dancing.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its players use accordions and fiddles for Scottish social dancing” describes Scottish dance band, not Steel band. Different sizes of pan provide melody, harmony and bass parts.

questions.question: A performer makes an instrument sound with a hand, stick, mallet or beater. What is this?
questions.hint: A hand, stick or mallet makes sudden contact.
questions.explanation: A performer makes an instrument sound with a hand, stick, mallet or beater.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Striking / hitting?

answers.text: A hand, stick, mallet or beater hits the instrument.
Air is directed into or across an instrument to begin its sound.
It is the main playing action for many percussion instruments.

questions.hint: Air travels into or across the instrument.
questions.explanation: The statement “Air is directed into or across an instrument to begin its sound” describes Blowing, not Striking / hitting. A performer makes an instrument sound with a hand, stick, mallet or beater.

questions.question: The sound comes from vibrations made by bowing or plucking. What is this?
questions.hint: Bows and fingers set this orchestral family vibrating.
questions.explanation: The sound comes from vibrations made by bowing or plucking.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Strings?

answers.text: Violin, viola, cello, double bass and harp are members.
Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a reed.
Bowing and plucking are common playing methods.

questions.hint: Flute air edges, clarinet reeds and oboe double reeds.
questions.explanation: The statement “Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a reed” describes Woodwind, not Strings. The sound comes from vibrations made by bowing or plucking.

questions.question: A finger or plectrum sweeps quickly across several strings, usually to play a chord. What is this?
questions.hint: One sweeping hand movement sounds a whole guitar chord.
questions.explanation: A finger or plectrum sweeps quickly across several strings, usually to play a chord.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Strumming?

answers.text: A finger or plectrum sweeps across several strings quickly.
It usually sounds a chord on guitar or a similar instrument.
Several strings sound in one sweeping action.
One string is pulled and released to make a single clear attack.

questions.hint: Pull and release one string with a finger or plectrum.
questions.explanation: The statement “One string is pulled and released to make a single clear attack” describes Plucking, not Strumming. A finger or plectrum sweeps quickly across several strings, usually to play a chord.

questions.question: Percussion instruments that play clear notes, scales or melodies. What is this?
questions.hint: Mallet instruments that can play a melody.
questions.explanation: Percussion instruments that play clear notes, scales or melodies.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Tuned percussion?

answers.text: Its sounds have no fixed musical pitch and are heard mainly as rhythm.
Its instruments produce definite, identifiable pitches.
They can play individual notes, scales or melodies.
Timpani, glockenspiel and xylophone are examples.

questions.hint: Rhythm and colour without fixed pitches.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its sounds have no fixed musical pitch and are heard mainly as rhythm” describes Untuned percussion, not Tuned percussion. Percussion instruments that play clear notes, scales or melodies.

questions.question: Percussion instruments with no fixed musical pitch. What is this?
questions.hint: Rhythm and colour without fixed pitches.
questions.explanation: Percussion instruments with no fixed musical pitch.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Untuned percussion?

answers.text: Its instruments have no fixed musical pitch.
Its instruments can play named notes, scales and melodies.
Their sounds are heard mainly as rhythm and tone colour.
Snare drum, cymbals, triangle and tambourine are examples.

questions.hint: Mallet instruments that can play a melody.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its instruments can play named notes, scales and melodies” describes Tuned percussion, not Untuned percussion. Percussion instruments with no fixed musical pitch.

questions.question: The sound made when a person sings or speaks. What is this?
questions.hint: The instrument every singer carries in their throat.
questions.explanation: The sound made when a person sings or speaks.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Voice?

answers.text: It is the human sound source for speaking and singing.
Vibrating vocal folds create its pitch.
Vibrating strings are amplified naturally by a hollow wooden body.
The mouth and body resonances shape its sound.

questions.hint: Six strings and a hollow wooden body—no electricity.
questions.explanation: The statement “Vibrating strings are amplified naturally by a hollow wooden body” describes Acoustic guitar, not Voice. The sound made when a person sings or speaks.

questions.question: An orchestral family played by blowing across an opening or through a single or double reed. What is this?
questions.hint: Flute air edges, clarinet reeds and oboe double reeds.
questions.explanation: An orchestral family played by blowing across an opening or through a single or double reed.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Woodwind?

answers.text: Sound begins by blowing across an opening or through a reed.
Flute, oboe, clarinet, bassoon and saxophone are members.
Modern members may be made from wood or metal.
Buzzing lips make the sound through a metal mouthpiece.

questions.hint: Buzzing lips meet a cup-shaped mouthpiece.
questions.explanation: The statement “Buzzing lips make the sound through a metal mouthpiece” describes Brass, not Woodwind. An orchestral family played by blowing across an opening or through a single or double reed.
